title: Linear-optical test of quantum contextuality with sequential measurements
authors: Jiaqi Liu, Bita Olamaei, Lijian Zhang, Ali Asadian, Saleh Rahimi-Keshari
abstract: Quantum contextuality provides a fundamental signature of nonclassical behavior that cannot be explained by noncontextual hidden-variable models. We propose and experimentally implement a linear-optical setup for demonstrating Kochen-Specker contextuality via a violation of the KCBS inequality using single photons. Our scheme employs sequential measurements realized with linear-optical networks and on-off photodetectors. The construction ensures that each co-measured observable is implemented by the same physical operation across different contexts. Our experimental results demonstrate a clear violation of the KCBS inequality and robustness against photon loss. Beyond fundamental investigations, the proposed setup provides a practical tool for probing non-classicality and photon-number statistics of quantum states, which in turn enables the verification of single-photon sources.
summary: The paper presents an experimental implementation of a Kochen-Specker contextuality test using sequential measurements in a linear-optical setup. By utilizing 'no-click' detection events, the researchers successfully demonstrated a violation of the KCBS inequality. The setup also functions as a practical tool for verifying the quality of single-photon sources and probing photon-number statistics.
main_problem: Demonstrating Kochen-Specker quantum contextuality in a linear-optical setup using sequential measurements while ensuring that the same observable is implemented identically across different contexts.
main_result: The authors experimentally demonstrated a clear violation of the KCBS inequality with a value of 2.2363 ± 0.0041 and showed that the setup can serve as a tool for verifying single-photon sources and photon-number statistics.
method: A sequential measurement scheme is implemented using linear-optical networks and on-off photodetectors, where 'no-click' events are used to preserve the photon for subsequent measurements.
model_or_system: A single-photon system distributed among M bosonic modes, implemented via a linear-optical setup using a pulsed Ti:SAG laser, BBO crystals for SPDC, and a hybrid encoding of spatial paths and polarization.
key_observables: The KCBS parameter S, which quantifies the violation of the Kochen-Specker inequality.
important_parameters: The single-photon population density (rho_11), photon-loss rate (rho_00), and the amplitude of coherent states (alpha).
main_assumptions: The fair-sampling assumption and the trusted-device assumption for the experimental components.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the experimental setup and the sequential measurement scheme; Figure 2 shows the physical components like beam displacers and wave plates; Figure 3 depicts the dependence of the KCBS quantity on the photon-loss rate.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of contextuality, describes the proposed sequential measurement scheme and mathematical framework, details the experimental implementation and setup, presents the results of the KCBS violation and robustness to loss, and concludes with applications for photon-number verification.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum information as it provides a practical method for certifying non-classicality and single-photon statistics using a robust, loss-tolerant sequential measurement protocol.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Setup for the implementation of the KCBS non- contextuality test. Our system consists of a single photon distributed over M modes, which can model any quantum sys- tem with an M-dimensional Hilbert space. Measurement A(j)

FIG. 2. Experimental setup. A pulsed Ti:Sapphire laser is frequency doubled via parametric up-conversion in the first β- barium borate (BBO) crystal; the resulting second harmonic then pumps a second BBO crystal to generate photon pairs via spontaneous parametric down-conversion. One photon is routed through a Glan polarizer and a polarizing beam splitter (PBS) into the optical path, while the other (heralding photon) is directly coupled into a single-photon counting module (SPCM). The device within the dashed box is used to simulate photon loss in the optical path. FIG. 3. The extent of violation of the KCBS inequality is quantified by how much the KCBS value S exceeds the clas- sical bound of 2. The dependence of this quantity on the photon-loss rate is shown, where values above and below 2 correspond to violation and adherence to the inequality, re- spectively. Here, the “Ideal S Value” denotes the theoretical prediction S(ρ1) for the input state ρ1 = ρ00|0⟩⟨0|+ρ11|1⟩1⟨1|, where ρ00 represents the photon-loss rate. The “Experimental S Value” corresponds to the measured data obtained in our experiment. The “Classical Bound” indicates the threshold value in the inequality that separates the classical and quan- tum regimes.
